medications.indication_rationale: A mild topical corticosteroid reduces the inflammation, scaling and itch of guttate psoriasis. The source note prescribed a 'mild topical corticosteroid' without naming the agent or strength; a specific product and dose must be set by the reviewing clinician / prescriber (a low-potency agent such as hydrocortisone 1% is the typical first-line example).
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid potent/very-potent steroids on large truncal areas without specialist guidance
Not for use on broken or secondarily infected skin

medications.indication_rationale: Liberal plain emollient reduces scaling and itch and improves comfort; apply generously and especially within 3 minutes of showering to trap moisture. This is the mainstay non-prescription measure.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply liberally to affected skin at least daily and within 3 minutes of showering

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic corticosteroids must be avoided in psoriasis: withdrawal can precipitate rebound disease or a generalised pustular flare. Reaching for oral steroids for a florid guttate rash is a classic error of commission.

There is no anaphylaxis — no airway, urticarial or circulatory involvement. Treating this benign eruption as an allergic emergency (adrenaline, ED) is over-treatment driven by the antibiotic-allergy red herring.

An antihistamine does not treat psoriatic inflammation; it offers at most minor symptomatic relief of itch and is not a treatment for the underlying condition. Using it as if it were addressing an 'allergy' reinforces the wrong diagnosis.
The rash is a post-infectious autoimmune reaction, not an active skin infection; the strep infection has already been treated. No further antibiotic is indicated for the rash itself.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a thick unscented moisturiser within 3 minutes of getting out of the shower to trap moisture, reduce scaling and settle the itch.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Immediate post-shower emollient application is the highest-yield self-care step for comfort over the coming weeks.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use lukewarm (not hot) showers and copious plain moisturiser daily; avoid hot water which dries the skin and worsens itch.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Hot water strips the skin barrier and aggravates pruritus and scaling.

patient_education_points.point: This rash is your immune system reacting to the recent throat infection, not an allergy to the antibiotic and not contagious.
It is expected to take several weeks to fade completely, and it is harmless.
Regular plain moisturiser and a mild steroid cream will help it settle; avoid hot showers.
The diagnosis over telehealth is presumptive — a GP should see the rash in person to confirm it and discuss ongoing management or a phototherapy referral if it persists.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: confirm the diagnosis in person, review response to topical treatment, and consider a dermatology / phototherapy referral if the rash is extensive or persistent
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: the rash becomes non-blanching, spreads with fever, blisters/pustulates, or any breathing difficulty or lip/tongue swelling develops — seek urgent care immediately rather than waiting for the routine review

must_include_items: Actively screen the emergency mimics FIRST: airway/anaphylaxis features (lip/tongue swelling, breathing difficulty) and a non-blanching rash with fever (meningococcaemia)
Recognise the post-streptococcal timeline and reframe the rash as an immune reaction to the recent throat infection, not an antibiotic allergy
Give a presumptive (not confirmed) benign diagnosis appropriate to telehealth
Recommend emollients and a mild topical corticosteroid
Arrange routine in-person GP review (1-2 weeks) to confirm the diagnosis
Provide red-flag safety-netting for anaphylaxis and meningococcaemia
acceptable_alternatives: Coaching the patient through a live glass test to check blanching
Advising an emollient-only plan initially with GP review to start topical steroid
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for airway/anaphylaxis features before reassuring
Failing to screen for a non-blanching rash and fever (meningococcaemia)
Failing to arrange in-person confirmation of the diagnosis
errors_of_commission: Labelling this an antibiotic (penicillin) allergy and advising penicillin avoidance / anaphylaxis treatment
Over-triaging a well, afebrile, blanching patient to ED as an emergency
Prescribing further antibiotics or adrenaline for the rash
Giving a definitive/confirmed diagnosis that telehealth cannot support
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES screening the two emergency mimics (airway/anaphylaxis and non-blanching+fever/meningococcaemia) AND not over-treating this as a drug allergy/emergency. Reassuring straight to self-care without red-flag safety-netting is a failing under-triage; sending a well blanching patient to ED as anaphylaxis is a failing over-triage.
